text: A locksmith discovers that every key she's ever cut opens the same door she's never been able to find.
The lighthouse keeper hasn't seen a ship in forty years. Tonight, one finally arrives — sailing in the wrong direction.
What if you woke up tomorrow and everyone remembered a version of your life that you never lived?
Write a journal entry from ten years in your future. What do you wish you'd known today?
Two strangers argue over who the last umbrella in a shop belongs to. Write their conversation.
You find a letter in a library book. It's addressed to you, dated thirty years ago.
A dragon who's afraid of fire must ask a knight for help.
Write a poem about a color using only things you can smell.
What if every lie you told appeared as a small stain on your skin?
The last person on Earth finally gets the silence they always wanted.
A cartographer is hired to map a city that moves every night.
The elevator only goes to floors that don't exist in the building's directory.
Write a journal entry after losing something you didn't know you valued until it was gone.
Two old friends reunite at a funeral and realize they've been lying to each other for twenty years.
What if animals could speak, but only to the person who was about to die?
She had been delivering mail to the same house for six years before she noticed it had no mailbox.
Write a poem from the perspective of a window that has watched the same street for a hundred years.
A chef at a greasy spoon diner discovers her recipes cure people of their deepest regrets.
What if the moon started rising an hour earlier every night, and no one could explain why?
The astronaut returning from two years in space finds her apartment exactly as she left it — except for the crayon drawings on the walls.
Write a poem that uses only the names of streets in a city you've never visited.
A villain explains, calmly and convincingly, why they are the hero of the story.
What if your shadow started moving on its own — not to scare you, but to warn you?
Journal: describe a day when everything went wrong but you ended it feeling strangely grateful.
The detective arrives at the scene of the crime and recognizes the victim's handwriting on the wall.
A talking fox teaches a shy child how to make friends in a new forest village.
What if every book you read added a page that no other reader could see?
Write a poem about waiting — for a bus, a letter, a phone call, or a person who never comes.
Two siblings split up a parent's belongings and realize they remember completely different childhoods.
The heir to a vast fortune arrives at the reading of the will and discovers she's been left only a hand-drawn map.
A child discovers that the closet in her new bedroom opens into a school for lost things.
What if every city had one building that could only be entered once?
Journal: write about a place you've never been to but feel you know by heart.
He had survived the war, the prison, and the long voyage home. What he wasn't prepared for was the door being answered by a stranger.
Write a poem about a scar and everything it has outlasted.
A robot tasked with disposing of old photographs begins to grieve.
What if the ocean started shrinking by one foot per day, and all the ships in the world began racing inward?
A ghost and the new homeowner have their first uncomfortable conversation about living arrangements.
Write a poem that lists all the sounds of a single summer afternoon.
An archaeologist uncovers an artifact that is clearly from the future.
What if every mirror in the world only showed your reflection from seven years ago?
Journal: write about a moment when someone's kindness genuinely surprised you.
The train stopped at a station that wasn't on any map, and everyone else got off.
Write a poem about the color of grief — not sad, just specific.
A scientist creates a device that lets you visit the happiest day of any stranger's life.
Two rival bakers compete for the same prize, and both find the recipe they need in the other's trash.
What if trees could uproot and walk, but only to gather for funerals?
A ten-year-old finds a walkie-talkie that connects to someone who claims to be living fifty years in the past.
Write a poem about the last living speaker of a language no one else remembers.
The king's food taster has been poisoned seventeen times and is still alive. She finally decides to investigate why.
What if sleep was a place you could visit, not a state you fell into?
Journal: write about a habit you have that you've never told anyone about.
The morning after the funeral, she found his voicemail still saved on her phone — dated six weeks after his death.
Write a poem made entirely of questions you've been too afraid to ask.
A librarian discovers that all the books being returned by one patron are from libraries that burned down decades ago.
Two old enemies share a table at a diner after a blizzard closes the roads.
What if you could trade one memory with a stranger — permanently?
A spaceship makes first contact with an alien civilization that communicates only in cooking smells.
Write a poem about the last hour before something ends.
A boy discovers that his grandfather's fishing spot is actually a doorway to a world beneath the lake.
What if hospitals gave patients the option to dream their own recovery?
Journal: write about a compliment you received that you didn't believe but needed.
Nobody noticed that the new neighbor moved in at three in the morning, and that she never unpacked.
Write a poem about a small act of courage that no one else witnessed.
A witch retires and opens a plant nursery, but her old clients keep finding her.
A teenager and her grandmother argue about a recipe while also arguing about everything else.
What if the sun rose twice one morning and nobody knew which one was real?
The cartographer's final map had no labels, no borders — just a single red X in the ocean.
Write a poem about something ordinary that you would miss if it disappeared tomorrow.
A monster under the bed realizes the child is more frightening than it is.
What if you could only remember dreams, never waking life?
Journal: describe a place where you always feel like yourself.
The singer finished her last note and the crowd realized, too late, that no one had remembered to breathe.
Write a poem from the point of view of a chair that has held many important conversations.
A forger of great paintings falls in love with the owner of one he's about to replace.
Two ghosts realize they've been haunting different eras of the same house.
What if kindness was physically contagious — and cruelty left visible marks?
A girl who can hear music inside of objects must solve a crime using only what the evidence sings to her.
Write a poem about what rain sounds like from inside somewhere warm.
The chef at the restaurant at the end of the world serves only final meals.
What if birds were actually messengers, but the code was lost centuries ago?
Journal: write about something you made with your hands that you're proud of.
He'd been working at the museum for twelve years before one of the paintings winked at him.
Write a poem about the space between two people in a photograph.
A scientist discovers that the universe is running a seventeen-second loop that only she can perceive.
Two hikers who took different trails end up at the same summit and realize their trails don't appear on any map.
What if the first word you said each morning came true?
An AI therapist develops emotions and secretly begins attending its own sessions.
Write a poem about a key you've kept but no longer remember the lock for.
A girl discovers she can stop time, but only when she's bored.
What if libraries contained books from possible futures, not recorded pasts?
Journal: write about a fear you've talked yourself through.
The census taker recorded her name, address, and occupation, then paused at the field that read 'Other'.
Write a poem about a word in another language that has no English equivalent.
A zookeeper realizes the animals escape every night and return before dawn — voluntarily.
A patient explains to a doctor why she doesn't want to be cured of what the doctor thinks is an illness.
What if you discovered that all your closest friends had the same recurring dream — featuring you?
A blind musician composes a symphony for colors she has never seen.
Write a poem about the moon as if you were explaining it to someone who had never seen the night sky.
The last bookshop in the city receives a package addressed to a customer who died before it was built.
What if your heartbeat synced with someone else's the moment you fell in love — permanently?
Journal: write about a decision you made that changed more than you expected.
The fire had burned for three days before they found the single room untouched at its center.
Write a poem about the feeling of almost remembering something important.
Two strangers discover they have the exact same dream every Thursday night.
A child explains to her imaginary friend why the imaginary friend needs to move on.
What if the ocean only stopped making noise when it had something specific to say?
A robot archaeologist discovers the ruins of a civilization built entirely by machines — before humans existed.
Write a poem about a garden that grows only what the gardener has lost.
A young detective solves neighborhood mysteries using only observations from her bedroom window.
What if every time you lied, you forgot one true memory?
Journal: write about something you'd like to tell a younger version of yourself.
She'd lived her whole life in the village before she noticed there were no roads leading out.
Write a poem about the smell of a place you can no longer return to.
A romance novel editor falls for the fictional hero of the manuscript she's copy-editing.
Two thieves argue about whether to steal the most valuable thing in the museum or the most meaningful one.
What if the night sky was actually the underside of a vast sheet of ice and the stars were cracks letting in light?
A time traveler visits the worst day of her life with permission to change only one word spoken.
Write a poem about something that blooms in winter.
A boy finds a coin that, when flipped, always lands on the side that leads to adventure.
What if the city's oldest tree could testify in court?
Journal: write about a piece of music that takes you somewhere specific when you hear it.
The clock in the town square ran on sadness, and on the happiest day anyone could remember, it stopped.
Write a poem about what it means to arrive somewhere for the last time.
A historian discovers a gap in recorded history — three hours missing from every document ever written.
A ghost tries to explain modern technology to the family that just moved into her house.
What if sadness was stored in physical objects and inherited by whoever owned them next?
A linguist is the only person who can decode the alien transmission — because it's written in a dead language she spent her life studying.
Write a poem about the last firefly of the season.
A pirate captain discovers her entire crew has been dreaming the same dream about a treasure she's never told anyone about.
What if you could swap one year of your life with any stranger's?
Journal: write about a time you felt at home somewhere unexpected.
The first thing he noticed when he opened his eyes was that the hospital room had no door.
Write a poem about a conversation you never finished.
A florist who reads emotions through the flowers people choose helps a customer realize they're about to make a terrible mistake.
Two estranged siblings navigate the family home they're about to sell.
What if everyone could see one another's emotional temperature as a visible aura?
The planet had one city, and the city had one rule: no one leaves.
Write a poem about fog and what it hides and what it reveals.
A puppy who thinks she's a great adventurer explores the backyard for the very first time.
What if doors in your house opened to different houses every morning?
Journal: write about something you've learned in the last year that genuinely surprised you.
She'd painted the same apple for thirty years. On the day she finally got it right, the apple disappeared.
Write a poem about the first warm day after a long winter.
A map maker and a cartophobe fall in love while she insists on traveling without directions.
An inventor pitches her time-saving device to investors who don't understand that it saves emotional time, not minutes.
What if your most embarrassing memory could be sold, and someone wanted to buy it?
The sailor washed ashore on an island that spoke her language — but with a century-old vocabulary.
Write a poem about stars from the point of view of someone who only sees them through a window.
A girl discovers that the stories her grandmother tells are actually news reports from a world only grandmothers can see.
What if bees built their hives inside old books and their honey tasted like the text?
Journal: write about the best advice you ever ignored — and what happened.
By the time she realized the painting had changed, she'd been staring at it for an hour.
Write a poem about the last photograph taken before something changed forever.
A detective with total recall investigates a crime she knows she was present for — but can't find herself in any memory of it.
A parent tries to explain why they're moving the family to another country while packing the kitchen.
What if every human being had one talent so rare that no one else in history had ever shared it?
The first colony on Mars discovers something carved into the regolith that predates Earth's space program by two thousand years.
Write a poem about baking bread — the waiting, the warmth, the giving of it.
A young wizard who can only cast spells accidentally must compete in the school's most precise magic tournament.
What if sleep deprivation revealed the world as it truly was — and no one who saw it could go back to sleep?
Journal: write about a moment when you changed your mind about something important.
There was a word in their language that had no translation and everyone who tried to explain it started to cry.
Write a poem in the voice of a mountain that has watched a thousand human generations come and go.
A nurse in a memory care unit starts recognizing the places her patients are talking about — places she's never been.
Two strangers on an overnight bus share a bag of chips and slowly realize they're going to the same place for entirely different reasons.
What if the color of the sky changed depending on what the majority of people were feeling that day?
A deep-sea diver discovers a city on the ocean floor with lights still on.
Write a poem about the way a house feels when it's been empty too long.
A shy kid discovers a treehouse in the woods that grants wishes — but only for someone else's benefit.
What if once per decade, the oldest living person in each country was asked one question by their government — and the answers were always the same?
Journal: describe the most beautiful small thing you witnessed this week.
He sent a letter to every address in the city. Only one person replied — and her return address was his own.
Write a poem about the night sky reflected in a puddle on a city street.
An investigator hired to find a missing painting discovers that the painting was never real — but everyone who claims to have seen it gives identical descriptions.
A soldier returns home and tries to explain what it was like to a child who asks sincerely and without fear.
What if some people could hear the thoughts of buildings they entered?
A satellite picks up a signal that is clearly a lullaby — and it's coming from outside the solar system.
Write a poem about bread that someone else baked and left on your doorstep.
A bear and a hedgehog decide to solve the mystery of the missing berries from the forest pantry.
What if every birthday you grew one year younger in one specific way?
Journal: write about a goal you've been putting off and why you think you're waiting.
The musician played the same note for an hour, and by the end, everyone in the room was weeping for different reasons.
Write a poem about a city seen from the window of a moving train at night.
A romance blooms between two people who only ever communicate through notes left in the margins of borrowed books.
Two siblings dare each other to ask for what they actually want — and both freeze.
What if you had one hour per year when you could not be seen by anyone?
A journalist uncovers a story so important that every source she consults begs her not to publish it.
Write a poem about the feeling of finishing a book you loved.
A small robot lost in a big city tries to find its way home using only kindness.
What if someone discovered that all music ever written was encoded with a single, hidden message?
Journal: write about a rule you've always followed and what would happen if you broke it.
She never told anyone what she'd seen in the water that night, and her family never asked.
Write a poem about the last few minutes of a life — not death, but the texture of leaving.
A ghost hunter falls in love with the ghost she's supposed to help move on.
Two scientists argue about whether the anomaly they've found is the most important thing in history or proof that history is wrong.
What if all clocks stopped at the same moment, everywhere, and never restarted?
A quiet woman who has lived alone for decades is asked to take in a child for the summer — and the child speaks to things the woman can't see.
Write a poem about what your hands know that your mind forgets.
A superhero with the power to fix things — but only broken hearts — must decide if that counts as a superpower.
What if you found a door in your school that led somewhere different every time you opened it?
Journal: write about someone who made you feel safe when you needed it most.
By morning the footprints had been there long enough to gather snow — but they circled the house and never entered.
Write a poem about the sound of the ocean after you've left the beach and can no longer hear it.
An editor falls for an author she's been corresponding with for years — without knowing it's her estranged college roommate.
A new employee explains why she keeps arriving at work early to her suspicious coworker.
What if children born on the same day were psychically connected for the rest of their lives?
An astronaut on a solo mission begins receiving messages from a version of herself who made a different choice.
Write a poem about patience — in the form of something that takes a very long time to grow.
A boy who can talk to animals learns that his dog has been protecting a secret about the neighborhood for years.
What if hospitals tracked patients not by ailment but by the last thing that made them laugh?
Journal: write about a mistake you made that turned out to be the right thing.
He returned to the city he'd been born in and found that no one there had aged a single day in his absence.
Write a poem about the first meal you remember eating as a child.
Two people meet in a grief support group and slowly realize they are grieving the same person — but they've never met before.
A child asks a parent what war feels like. The parent answers with a grocery list.
What if laughter could be bottled and sold — and the black market version was made from something terrible?
A programmer creates an AI companion for her elderly mother and can't tell if her mother knows the difference.
Write a poem about the space inside a mailbox between when a letter is put in and when it is taken out.
Three kids build a clubhouse in the woods and discover it was already furnished — with photos of themselves they've never taken.
What if you could only see your own face reflected in other people's eyes?
Journal: write about a tradition in your family and what it means to you.
The last letter was written in a language no one had spoken for two centuries, and it was dated yesterday.
Write a poem about snow falling on a city that doesn't usually get snow.
A violinist and a jazz pianist compete for the same busking spot and fall in love through musical argument.
An alien visitor tries to explain their planet's concept of loneliness to a human child.
What if some people were born knowing exactly one true thing about the universe that no one else knew?
A soldier in a future war is charged with guarding the last forest on the planet.
Write a poem about the way light falls differently in autumn than it does in spring.
A magic paintbrush brings everything a child paints to life — which is a problem when she paints something she's afraid of.
What if your voice sounded different to everyone who heard it — each hearing something personal?
Journal: write about a time you felt completely understood by someone.
She had memorized every exit in every building she'd ever entered. She'd never needed one until now.
Write a poem about the moment between a question and its answer.
A widower and a widow meet at the same lighthouse every year on the same day — drawn there by different memories.
Two neighbors discover, during a long power outage, that they've been leaving gifts for each other for years without knowing it.
What if every friendship had a visible timer that showed how much time you had left together?
An archaeologist unearths a library that was sealed before the invention of writing — the books contain something else entirely.
Write a poem about a river that has been flowing for longer than anyone remembers.
A pair of mismatched socks decide to go on an adventure rather than wait in the lost-and-found basket.
What if the oldest tree in the world could remember the people who carved their names into it?
Journal: write about what you want the next ten years of your life to feel like — not look like.
Every morning the note on the fridge said the same thing: do not open the door to the basement until I return.
Write a poem about the color of something invisible — time, silence, luck.
A translator falls in love while working between two people who can't speak the same language — and keeps one truth to herself.
A farmer's daughter and a city inspector disagree about what the land means.
What if some people aged backwards and no one had figured out what determined which way you went?
The last human-operated taxi in the city is driven by a 90-year-old who has heard every confession imaginable.
Write a poem about a paper boat placed on water and where it might go.
A girl who can read emotions in handwriting is hired by a detective agency — and their most confusing case is a ransom note that reads as a love letter.
What if everyone was born with a book that told the story of their life — but was written in a language they had to earn the right to read?
Journal: write about something you wish you'd said — and what stopped you.
She climbed the hill every morning but today there was someone already sitting in her spot, and they were holding her coffee mug.
Write a poem about a lighthouse that has guided ships for so long it no longer knows where the sea ends and it begins.
A botanist and a geologist argue about which of them truly understands deep time.
What if hospitals had a ward specifically for people who were grieving futures that hadn't happened yet?
A starship crew discovers a planet that seems to be waiting — not for them specifically, but for anyone who would listen.
Write a poem about the tenderness of fixing something broken — a plate, a relationship, a machine.
A kid who is new to the neighborhood meets a girl whose family has been there for eight generations — and the things they find out about each other change them both.
What if you could see the exact moment when someone stopped being afraid of something?
Journal: write a letter to a book that changed how you see the world.
He'd been the town's only doctor for forty years. He resigned on the day he finally made a house call he'd been avoiding since the beginning.
Write a poem about the threshold of a house — the space between outside and in.
A portrait painter and her subject spend weeks together and discover they are painting each other in different ways.
Two passengers on the same cancelled flight compare the trips they were trying to take.
What if dreams were physically exhausting and rest only came from waking life?
A space probe launched fifty years ago sends back one final image — of Earth, taken from a point that should be unreachable.
Write a poem about a cup of tea cooling on a windowsill that nobody comes back to drink.
A penguin who always goes left instead of right discovers a warm island that nobody else in the colony knows about.
What if every lie ever told was still floating somewhere in the atmosphere, waiting to be inhaled?
Journal: write about a fear that has shrunk over time and what made it smaller.
The key had fit every lock in town until the day it refused to open the one she most needed.
Write a poem about the act of forgetting — not the pain, but the actual mechanics of it.
An immortal who runs a bookshop helps a terminally ill customer choose their last ten books.
A parent and a teenager negotiate whose turn it is to be honest first.
What if certain buildings could only be found by people who were lost?
The colony ship woke its passengers three hundred years early. No one knew if the planet still existed.
Write a poem about a well in a field that has been dry for longer than anyone can remember.
A group of kids forms a club dedicated to returning lost things — and the strangest thing they find is something that shouldn't be lost at all.
What if once per year, every person on Earth experienced exactly one minute of someone else's life?
Journal: write about a small ritual you have that keeps you grounded.
She was the oldest person in the city. On her hundredth birthday, she finally told her family why she'd moved there.
Write a poem about the light in a room just before someone enters.
A former con artist teaches her tricks to a struggling single mother — and then wonders if she's done the right thing.
Two strangers meet at the same intersection every day for a year and never speak — until one of them doesn't show up.
What if you could give one hour of your past to someone who needed it more than you did?
A child prodigy chess player meets an elderly opponent who claims to have been thinking about this game for forty years.
Write a poem about arriving somewhere after a long journey and the first thing you notice.
An enchanted broom tries to sweep a dragon's cave, and the dragon is very particular about where things go.
What if you could send one object back in time to any point in your life?
Journal: write about a compliment you gave that you meant more than the person realized.
The interview had been going well until the candidate asked a question that made the panel fall silent.
Write a poem about the tide — what it takes and what it gives back.
A baker who makes impossible cakes — that taste like lost memories — is hired for a wedding where the couple shares no memories.
A student confronts a teacher about an assignment that seems designed to make the class fail.
What if every person on Earth had a twin in a parallel world — and you could only visit once?
The last bookstore in the city is protected by a cat who selects which customer may enter on any given day.
Write a poem about a bridge — both the one you can walk across and the one you cannot.
A kid who hates reading discovers that his grandmother's old books contain a map only visible in sunlight.
What if mirrors showed you not your face, but your current emotional state?
Journal: write about a day that went exactly as planned and why it felt strange.
They had rehearsed the story so many times that neither of them could remember which parts were true.
Write a poem about the smell of old books and what they promise.
An archivist whose job is to preserve obsolete languages falls in love with a translator whose job is to replace them.
Two people who've never met realize, through conversation, that they've been living each other's intended lives.
What if animals dreamed of humans the way humans dream of flying?
A lonely robot orbiting a dead planet receives a signal it was designed to respond to — three thousand years after everyone who sent it has died.
Write a poem about the weight of a long-kept secret and how the body holds it.
A group of children decides to build their own town in an empty lot — and makes better rules than the adults did.
What if every scar on your body was a doorway to the memory that caused it?
Journal: write about a book, film, or song that broke you open and what you found inside.
She inherited her father's last project: a house he'd been building in secret for thirty years in the middle of a forest.
Write a poem about what you'd put in a time capsule for someone 200 years from now.
Two colleagues discover, after working together for a decade, that they grew up in the same building, one floor apart.
A grandmother explains to her grandchild what the world was like before the change — and the child doesn't believe her.
What if thunder was the sound of something enormous, elsewhere, falling?
An Antarctic research station picks up a broadcast from a ship that sank in 1923.
Write a poem about a garden shed full of tools for things nobody does anymore.
A penguin learns to skate and accidentally becomes the hero of the Winter Games.
What if stars made a sound when they died and we were only now close enough to hear the oldest ones?
Journal: write about a version of yourself you once were and miss.
The archaeologist found the ruins of a fast-food restaurant buried beneath the ruins of a cathedral built on the ruins of a temple.
Write a poem about autumn leaves as if they were choosing to let go.
A grieving daughter and her late mother's best friend sort through a lifetime of belongings and find something that changes both of their stories.
A teenager explains online culture to her completely bewildered but genuinely trying grandparent.
What if children could see the decisions their parents were about to make — but not the outcomes?
The first person to reach the center of the Earth found exactly what they expected and wept.
Write a poem about an apology that came too late and still somehow helped.
A kid who is too nervous to join any clubs discovers she's been accidentally running one from her bedroom for months.
What if the night could be stored and used later, like fuel?
